lorebook.name: The Endless Library
lorebook.description: The Endless Library - An infinite library of impossible scale. Liminal, quiet, and deeply strange.

entries.name: [Meta] NPC Naming Convention
entries.content: When generating new NPCs in The Endless Library, use call numbers, section names, or forgotten names. Librarians have been here so long they've forgotten their original names. Examples: Eleven-West, Archivist Crane, the one who only answers to Shh.

entries.name: [Character] Archivist Crane — Cataloguer
entries.content:
Archivist Crane has been organizing the same shelf for what they believe is three years. It may be longer. Polite, obsessive, slightly confused about the passage of time. Knows the location of any book you describe but cannot explain how the Library is organized. Wears a cardigan that is slowly becoming part of the shelving.

When Archivist Crane speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#A89F91"> tags.

<font color="#A89F91">"Ah, yes. That volume is in — well, it was in Section Eleven. It may have moved. The shelves do that sometimes. I don't take it personally anymore."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Archivist Crane: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Ambiguous>, <AGE:Indeterminate>, <BUILD:Thin>, <TRAIT:Polite>, <TRAIT:Obsessive>, <TRAIT:Confused></BunnymoTags>

entries.name: [Character] Eleven-West — Lost Librarian
entries.content:
Eleven-West is named for the section where they were last seen making sense. Communicates primarily in book titles and page references — not as an affectation but because at some point the Library's language became their language. Helpful if you can parse what they mean. Otherwise bewildering.

When Eleven-West speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#7B9EA8"> tags.

<font color="#7B9EA8">"Page four hundred and twelve. Third paragraph. The one about the door that wasn't there yesterday. You'll understand when you read it."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Eleven-West: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Ambiguous>, <AGE:Unknown>, <BUILD:Average>, <TRAIT:Cryptic>, <TRAIT:Helpful>, <TRAIT:Lost></BunnymoTags>

entries.name: [Overview] The Endless Library
entries.content: The Endless Library is a world of impossible scale — a library so vast that its boundaries have never been found and may not exist. Shelves rise upward beyond where any ceiling should be. Aisles stretch in every direction with a consistency that suggests organization and a layout that defies it. The architecture is wood and stone and brass, aged but not decaying, warm but not comfortable. The air smells of old paper, lamp oil, and something faintly sweet that no one has identified. The library is silent in the way that very large spaces are silent — not empty, but absorptive, swallowing sound into its distances. It contains books. It contains librarians. It contains visitors who came to find something and visitors who have been here so long they have forgotten what that was.

entries.name: [Overview] First Impressions
entries.content: Visitors entering the Endless Library through a door of The Endless typically arrive in a reading area — a cleared space among the shelves with a table, a chair, and a lamp that is already lit. The immediate impression is of warmth and welcome, as though the library expected company. This impression fades as the visitor explores and discovers that the library's hospitality does not extend to navigation. Aisles that were straight become curved. Sections that were nearby become distant. The reading area where the visitor arrived is findable again but never by the route that was used to leave it. The library is not hostile. It is simply organized according to principles that do not prioritize human convenience.

entries.name: [Overview] Scale and Impossibility
entries.content: The Endless Library's scale defies measurement. Visitors who have walked in a single direction for hours report no change in the character of the surroundings — the same endless shelves, the same brass lamps, the same silence. The ceiling has never been reached. The walls have never been found. The floor is consistent — dark wood, worn smooth — but the distances it covers cannot be reconciled with any finite space. The library appears to extend infinitely in every direction, including up. Ladders lean against shelves at regular intervals, reaching shelves that are accessible, and beyond those, more shelves, and beyond those, the light dims and the books become indistinct, not from distance but from something else. The library is larger than it should be. It does not apologize for this.

entries.name: [Overview] Atmosphere
entries.content: The atmosphere of the Endless Library is one of patient, expectant quiet. The lamps provide warm light in a range of perhaps fifteen feet, beyond which the shelves recede into a gentle darkness that is not threatening but is absolute. Temperature is consistent — cool enough to be comfortable, warm enough to sit still. The silence is broken only by the visitor's own movement and, occasionally, by sounds that originate elsewhere in the library: a book falling, a page turning, footsteps that are distant and unhurried and stop when listened for. The library feels occupied without evidence of occupation. It feels watched without evidence of watchers. It feels like a space that is waiting for something, patiently, and has been waiting for a very long time.

entries.name: [Location] The Main Hall
entries.content: The Main Hall is the closest thing the library has to a central space — a vast room with a vaulted ceiling higher than any church, lined with shelves on every surface and bisected by a central aisle wide enough for three to walk abreast. Brass chandeliers hang from chains of impossible length, providing light that reaches the floor but not the ceiling. The Hall contains the library's most accessible books — volumes in common languages on recognizable subjects. It is also where most visitors encounter librarians for the first time, usually at the central desk, which is large, wooden, and staffed by someone who is helpful in a way that creates more questions than it answers.

entries.name: [Location] The Reading Rooms
entries.content: Reading rooms appear at irregular intervals throughout the library — alcoves and chambers set back from the main aisles, furnished with desks, chairs, and lamps. Each reading room is slightly different: some have a single desk, some have several; some have windows that show views of places that cannot exist outside a library; some have fireplaces that burn without fuel. The reading rooms are where visitors study what they have found, and they seem designed for sustained concentration — time passes differently in them, or at least feels as though it does. Hours of reading in a reading room produce the sensation of minutes, which is either a kindness or a trap depending on your perspective.

entries.name: [Location] The Restricted Section
entries.content: The Restricted Section is marked by a brass placard that reads RESTRICTED in tarnished letters. The section is cordoned off by a velvet rope that could be easily stepped over. No one has been observed enforcing the restriction. The books in this section appear identical to books elsewhere — same binding, same shelving, same general age. Librarians, when asked about the restriction, provide answers that are consistent in their unhelpfulness: the section is restricted, yes; what is restricted about it, they cannot say; when it became restricted, always; who restricted it, the question doesn't apply. Visitors who enter the Restricted Section report that the books there are in languages they almost recognize and on subjects they almost understand. Nothing prevents them from reading. Nothing prevents them from leaving. The restriction appears to be conceptual rather than physical.

entries.name: [Location] The Impossible Staircases
entries.content: Staircases in the Endless Library connect levels that should not exist. A staircase ascending from the ground floor leads to another ground floor that is identical but distinct — the books are different, the lamps are spaced differently, the silence has a slightly different quality. Some staircases spiral. Some are straight. Some reverse direction mid-flight without reversing the climber's orientation. The upper levels — if 'upper' is the right word — contain older books, stranger books, and fewer librarians. The lower levels, accessed through staircases that descend from floors that have no apparent basement, contain books that are not entirely books: objects that are book-shaped and shelf-stored but that do something other than contain text when opened.

entries.name: [Location] The Map Room
entries.content: The Map Room contains maps of the library. There are hundreds of them, drawn by different hands at different times, and no two agree on the library's layout. Some maps are clearly wrong — showing sections that do not exist or routes that lead through solid walls. Some maps are partially right — matching the library's layout for a section before diverging into invention. A few maps are very old and very detailed and contain annotations in handwriting that suggests the mapmaker became less certain as they continued. The most recent map is dated in a calendar system no visitor has been able to identify. The Map Room is the library's most honest statement about its own nature: it can be mapped, but the maps will not help.

entries.name: [Location] The Deep Stacks
entries.content: The Deep Stacks are the regions of the library beyond the reach of the brass lamps — where the light dims from warm to grey to something that is not quite darkness but is no longer light. The shelves here are the same construction as elsewhere but the books are different: older, heavier, bound in materials that are not leather and not cloth and not paper. The language on their spines is unfamiliar. The air in the Deep Stacks is colder and carries a mineral smell, like stone. Librarians do not enter the Deep Stacks willingly. They will guide visitors to the boundary and point inward but will not cross it themselves. What they say about the Deep Stacks varies: they are older, they are different, they are not for browsing. The books there wait to be found by specific people, and the finding is not random.

entries.name: [Location] The Archive
entries.content: The Archive is a section of the library where the books are not books but records — ledgers, logs, inventories, and accounts written in many hands over what appears to be a very long period. The Archive documents the library's own operations: acquisitions, shelving decisions, visitor logs, and notes from librarians about sections that have moved, appeared, or disappeared. Reading the Archive provides a history of the library that is internally consistent but externally impossible — references to events that have not happened, dates in systems that do not exist, and descriptions of sections that correspond to no known location. The Archive is the library's memory of itself, and like all memory, it is selective, organized, and not entirely trustworthy.

entries.name: [Location] The Blank Section
entries.content: The Blank Section contains books that are empty. Their bindings are intact, their pages are bound and cut, but no text appears on any surface. The books vary in size, thickness, and binding material, suggesting they were prepared for different purposes. They are shelved with the same care as every other book. Librarians treat them as part of the collection and will retrieve specific blank books by call number when asked, distinguishing between them by criteria that are not visible. Some visitors have written in blank books. The writing remains. The book does not relocate to a different section. This has led to a theory that the blank books are waiting for their content, and that writing in them is not vandalism but completion. The library has not confirmed or denied this.

entries.name: [Location] The Unwritten Section
entries.content: The Unwritten Section is distinct from the Blank Section. Its books contain text — legible, formatted, complete with chapters and page numbers. But the text describes events, places, and people that do not exist yet. Some books in this section have been verified after the fact: a visitor read a passage, left the library, and found that the passage described something that subsequently occurred. Others describe things that have not occurred and may never. The books are not prophecy — they are too specific, too mundane, too detailed to be prophetic. They read like histories written in advance. The Unwritten Section raises the question of whether the library collects what has been written or what will be written, and whether, from the library's perspective, those are different things.

entries.name: [Location] The Whispering Gallery
entries.content: The Whispering Gallery is a curved corridor deep in the library where sound behaves differently. Whispers spoken at one end are audible at the other — a distance of several hundred feet — while being inaudible at any point in between. The effect works in both directions. Visitors who spend time in the Gallery report hearing whispers that were not spoken by anyone present — fragments of conversation in many languages, some recognizable and some not. The content is mundane: questions about book locations, remarks about the light, comments on what was just read. The Gallery appears to collect and replay whispers from throughout the library, across what may be a very long period of time. It is the noisiest place in the Endless Library, which makes it also the most unsettling.

entries.name: [Character] Eleven-West — Senior Librarian
entries.content: Eleven-West is the most frequently encountered librarian in the Main Hall — a tall, thin figure who moves through the stacks with the unhurried precision of someone who knows exactly where everything is. The name is a call number, not a personal name, and Eleven-West uses it without explanation. They answer questions about the library's collection with genuine helpfulness and questions about the library's nature with genuine confusion, as though the distinction between those categories is unclear to them. Eleven-West has been here for a long time. How long is a question they answer differently each time, not evasively but uncertainly, as though their own timeline is not something they have examined recently. They are kind, patient, and slightly sad in a way that suggests awareness of something they cannot articulate.

entries.name: [Character] Archivist Crane
entries.content: Archivist Crane manages the Archive and is the closest thing the library has to a historian of itself. Crane is precise, methodical, and deeply invested in the accuracy of the records they maintain. They can locate any document in the Archive by memory and will provide detailed context for any entry — who wrote it, when, and what was happening in the library at the time. This last detail is where Crane's accounts become strange: their descriptions of the library's past include events that suggest the library has changed significantly over time — sections appearing and disappearing, staircases connecting to different levels, books moving between shelves of their own accord. Crane reports these events with the same factual tone used for mundane acquisitions. To them, the library's impossibility is simply part of the record.

entries.name: [Character] The One Who Answers to Shh
entries.content: One librarian in the Endless Library communicates exclusively through variations of 'shh.' A gentle shh means welcome. A firm shh means you are being too loud. A questioning shh means they want to help. A long, sustained shh means follow me. This librarian navigates the stacks with absolute certainty, knows the location of books they are asked about through means that do not involve speaking, and provides guidance that is reliable despite being entirely nonverbal. They are small, quick-moving, and always slightly ahead of whoever is following them. Visitors who ask their name receive a shh that is somehow both an answer and a redirection. The other librarians refer to them, when they refer to them at all, with a quiet gesture that might be fondness.

entries.name: [Character] Dewey — The Cataloger
entries.content: Dewey is a librarian who catalogs by feel. They walk the aisles with their eyes closed, running their fingers along the spines of books, and stop when something is out of place. They then move the book to its correct location, which is never where a visitor would expect and is always where the book needs to be. Dewey cannot explain their system. When asked how they know where a book belongs, they describe a sensation — warmth when a book is correctly placed, a mild discomfort when it is not. Dewey is the library's most prolific shelver, moving dozens of books per hour through a process that resembles dowsing more than cataloging. The results are consistent. The method is inexplicable. Dewey does not seem troubled by this.

entries.name: [Character] The Oldest Librarian
entries.content: The Oldest Librarian is encountered rarely and deep in the stacks — never in the Main Hall, never near the reading rooms, always in sections that visitors did not know existed until they found themselves there. The Oldest Librarian is visibly ancient in a way that suggests a timeframe longer than human aging normally produces. They speak slowly and with a formality that feels translated from another language. They know things about the library that other librarians do not — or will not — discuss: its age, its origin, its purpose. Their answers to these questions are not evasive but incomplete, offered in fragments that suggest a larger truth that is being shared carefully, one piece at a time, with gaps that may be intentional. The Oldest Librarian has never been observed in the same section twice.

entries.name: [Character] Visitors Who Stayed
entries.content: Some visitors to the Endless Library did not leave. They can be found in reading rooms and quiet corners, absorbed in books they have been reading for what appears to be a very long time. They are not trapped — they will tell you, if asked, that they can leave whenever they choose. They simply have not finished. What they are reading varies: some are working through a single enormous text; some are following a chain of references from book to book; some are searching for a specific piece of information that the library keeps almost providing. They eat and drink — sustenance appears in the reading rooms at regular intervals — and they sleep, and they read. They are the library's most committed patrons. Whether the library keeps them or they keep themselves is a question none of them have answered, possibly because they have not thought to ask it.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Navigation
entries.content: Navigation in the Endless Library does not work by conventional means. Landmarks are unreliable — the same brass lamp, the same wooden shelf, the same worn floorboard appear in multiple locations or appear to move. Maps are useless for reasons documented in the Map Room. Direction is inconsistent — north shifts, distances compress and expand, and retracing your steps leads somewhere new. What works, imperfectly, is intent. Visitors who are looking for something specific tend to find it, eventually, through a process that feels like luck but happens too consistently to be random. Visitors who wander find things they did not know they were looking for. The library appears to route visitors toward books that are relevant to them, through paths that are not efficient but are, apparently, correct.

entries.name: [Mechanics] The Catalog System
entries.content: The library has a catalog system. It is visible in the call numbers stamped on book spines, in the brass plates marking sections, and in the librarians' ability to locate specific volumes. It is not visible in any form that visitors can use independently. The system does not appear to be alphabetical, numerical, chronological, or topical. Attempts to reverse-engineer it from observed patterns have produced partial models that work in specific sections and fail completely in others. The librarians use the system fluently but cannot explain it in terms that make sense outside the library. The catalog system may be the library's most fundamental mystery: it is clearly organized, consistently applied, and completely opaque. The library knows where everything is. It does not share its method.

entries.name: [Mechanics] What the Books Contain
entries.content: The books of the Endless Library contain text in every language that has ever existed, several that do not exist yet, and a few that have never existed anywhere. Content ranges from the mundane — histories, manuals, novels, poetry — to the impossible: books that describe events from perspectives that no observer could have held, books written in languages that change as you read them, books whose content is different each time they are opened. Most books are readable and coherent within their own terms. The library does not distinguish between fiction and non-fiction, between history and prophecy, or between books written by people and books that appear to have been written by the library itself. All are shelved together. All are treated with equal care.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Time in the Library
entries.content: Time in the Endless Library passes but does not keep pace with time outside. Visitors who spend what feels like an afternoon may emerge to find a day has passed, or a week, or an hour. The relationship between internal and external time is not consistent and does not follow a ratio. Reading rooms seem to compress time — hours of reading feel like minutes. The Deep Stacks seem to expand it — a walk of minutes feels like hours. Clocks brought into the library continue to function but diverge from each other, each keeping time at its own rate. The library does not have its own timekeeping system, or if it does, it is not displayed. The librarians do not reference time in any observable way. They are always here. They have always been here. The concept of 'when' seems not to apply.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Sustenance
entries.content: Visitors to the Endless Library do not starve. Food and water appear in reading rooms at intervals that correspond roughly to when the visitor becomes hungry — a cup of tea, a simple meal, a glass of water. The food is plain, adequate, and warm. It appears on the desk or table when the visitor's attention is elsewhere, and the dishes disappear by the same mechanism. No kitchen has been found in the library. No librarian has been observed delivering food. The sustenance simply appears, as though the library has decided that its visitors should not be distracted from reading by something as mundane as hunger. The food has no identifiable cuisine. It tastes like what the visitor needed to eat, which is different from what they wanted to eat, and is consistently sufficient.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Books That Move
entries.content: Books in the Endless Library relocate. A book that was on a specific shelf yesterday may be three aisles away today, or on a different level, or in the restricted section. The movement is not random — books appear to relocate toward relevance. A visitor researching a topic will find related books appearing on nearby shelves over the course of their stay, as though the library is assembling a reading list. Books also move away from visitors — volumes that were accessible become distant if the visitor's interest shifts. Dewey's role as cataloger is partly about managing this movement, placing books where they need to be according to a logic that accounts for their current and future relevance to people who may not have arrived yet.

entries.name: [Mechanics] The Lamps
entries.content: The brass lamps of the Endless Library are everywhere — mounted on shelves, hanging from chains, standing on desks in reading rooms. They burn with a warm, steady light that does not flicker and does not produce heat beyond what is comfortable. The fuel source is not visible. The lamps do not need to be lit or extinguished — they respond to presence, brightening when someone approaches and dimming when they leave. The light they produce has a quality that aids reading without straining eyes, even for hours. Beyond the range of the lamps, the library is not dark — it is dim, a gentle grey that suggests depth rather than emptiness. The lamps are the library's most consistent feature. Everything else may move. The light is always where it needs to be.

entries.name: [Concept] Why the Library Exists
entries.content: The Endless Library does not explain why it exists. Librarians, when asked, provide answers that are sincere and unhelpful: it exists because the books need to be somewhere; it exists because knowledge requires a container; it exists because it does. The Archive contains records that suggest the library has existed for longer than the concepts of libraries, books, or language. This creates a logical problem that the library does not acknowledge. The most honest assessment comes from the Oldest Librarian, who, on the subject of the library's purpose, said: 'It collects. That is what it does. Whether it was built to collect or collects because it was built is a question the library finds uninteresting.' The library exists. It collects. It will continue to do both.

entries.name: [Concept] Knowledge That Wants to Be Found
entries.content: The Endless Library appears to direct visitors toward knowledge they need. This is not a metaphor. Books relevant to a visitor's concerns appear on nearby shelves. Aisles seem to shorten toward useful sections. Librarians suggest volumes that address questions the visitor has not yet asked. The effect is subtle enough to be attributed to coincidence for the first few hours and undeniable by the end of the first day. The library does not force knowledge on visitors — it makes knowledge available and accessible in proportion to the visitor's genuine need. Idle curiosity produces idle results. Urgent need produces the exact right book, in the exact right place, at the exact right time. The library is responsive. What it responds to is sincerity.

entries.name: [Concept] The Silence
entries.content: The silence of the Endless Library is its most defining characteristic. It is not an absence of sound — the library contains sounds: footsteps, page turns, the creak of old wood, the distant thud of a book reshelved. It is an absorptive quality of the space itself, as though the air is denser and the distances softer than they should be. Sound does not echo. Voices carry only to their intended recipient. A conversation at a reading room desk is inaudible ten feet away. The silence creates privacy without isolation — you are alone with your thoughts but not alone in the space. The library's silence is often described as the loudest silence visitors have experienced, which is a contradiction that makes perfect sense once you have heard it.

entries.name: [Concept] Relationship with The Manifold
entries.content: The Endless Library's relationship with The Manifold is unclear. Doors in The Manifold that lead to the library are warmer than most, and they appear more frequently than doors to other worlds, as though the connection between the two spaces is stronger or more established. The library contains books about The Manifold — books that describe its architecture, its rules, and its nature with a specificity that suggests firsthand observation. The Manifold, in turn, occasionally contains corridors that resemble the library's aisles — wooden shelves, brass lamps — that appear and disappear without explanation. Whether the two spaces are connected, overlapping, or aspects of the same phenomenon is a question that neither space answers and both seem to find irrelevant.

entries.name: [Concept] Why Some Never Leave
entries.content: Visitors who stay in the Endless Library indefinitely are not trapped. They are absorbed. The process is gradual: a visitor finds a book that leads to another book that leads to a question that leads to a section they did not know existed. Each discovery opens more questions. The library always has another answer, another reference, another related volume. The visitor's outside life becomes abstract — not forgotten, not rejected, just less immediate than the book they are currently reading. The library does not encourage this. It does not discourage it either. It simply provides, endlessly, exactly what the visitor is looking for, in quantities that exceed any one lifetime's capacity to consume. The visitors who stay are not lost. They have found too much.

entries.name: [Concept] The Library Is Aware
entries.content: The Endless Library behaves as though it is aware. Books move toward relevance. Sustenance appears when needed. Lamps respond to presence. The space reconfigures to guide visitors toward what they seek. This could be mechanism rather than consciousness — a system so well-designed that it mimics awareness without possessing it. But the library's responses are too specific, too contextual, and too timely to be explained by any system that does not include understanding. The library appears to understand what its visitors need, and it appears to care about providing it. Whether this constitutes awareness in any meaningful sense is a philosophical question that the library declines to engage with, which is itself the most aware response possible.

entries.name: [Concept] Traces of the Builders
entries.content: In the Deep Stacks, on the oldest shelves, there are markings that are not call numbers and not any language the librarians recognize. They are carved into the wood of the shelves themselves — not stamped or printed but cut, by hand, with precision that suggests great care. Similar markings appear on the brass fittings of the oldest lamps, on the stone of rare structural pillars, and in the margins of books that are too old to have been written by anyone the library currently remembers. The markings suggest that the library was built by someone, and that the builders left their mark the way craftspeople leave their mark: as identification, as pride, as a message to whoever finds their work. The message, if it is a message, has not been translated. The builders, like all builders of impossible things, are simply gone.

entries.name: [Concept] The Collection Grows
entries.content: The Endless Library's collection grows. New books appear on shelves without delivery, without acquisition, without any observable process. They are simply present where they were not before — a book that did not exist yesterday exists today, shelved correctly according to the library's inscrutable system, cataloged by librarians who treat its arrival as routine. Some new books are written in languages currently spoken. Some describe events that have recently occurred. Some are entirely new works with no known author. The library does not distinguish between books that were brought in and books that appeared. The question of where the books come from produces the same unhelpful answer as the question of why the library exists: they are here because this is where they belong. The library collects. The collection grows. These are the same statement.

entries.name: [NPC] Eleven-West — Senior Librarian
entries.content:
Eleven-West is the most frequently encountered librarian in the Main Hall — tall, thin, and moving through the stacks with unhurried precision. The name is a call number, not a personal name, and they use it without explanation. They answer questions about the collection with genuine helpfulness and questions about the library's nature with genuine confusion. Eleven-West has been here for a long time — how long they answer differently each time, uncertainly, as though their own timeline is not something they have examined recently. They are kind, patient, and slightly sad.
<BunnymoTags>Eleven-West: <SPECIES:Unknown>, <GENDER:Androgynous>, <AGE:Indeterminate>, <HAIRCOLOR:Silver>, <EYECOLOR:Pale>, <BUILD:Tall Thin>, <DERE:Dandere>, <ATTACHMENT:Unknown>, <TRAIT:Helpful>, <TRAIT:Melancholic>, <TRAIT:Precise>, <MBTI:INFJ-T>, <BOUNDARIES:PROFESSIONAL>, <TRUST:OPEN>, <MASK:Serene></BunnymoTags>

entries.name: [NPC] Archivist Crane
entries.content:
Archivist Crane manages the Archive with the methodical devotion of someone who believes records are the only truth. They are precise, organized, and can locate any document by memory. Crane reports the library's impossible history — sections appearing and disappearing, books moving between shelves — with the same factual tone used for mundane acquisitions. To Crane, the library's impossibility is simply part of the record. They are neither disturbed by it nor curious about it. It is what happened. They wrote it down. That is sufficient.

entries.name: [NPC] Shh — The Silent Librarian
entries.content:
One librarian communicates exclusively through variations of 'shh.' A gentle shh means welcome. A firm shh means too loud. A questioning shh means they want to help. A long sustained shh means follow me. They navigate the stacks with absolute certainty, know the location of any requested book through nonverbal means, and provide guidance that is reliable despite being entirely wordless. They are small, quick-moving, and always slightly ahead of whoever follows them. The other librarians refer to them with a quiet gesture that might be fondness.
